message: The ANTISCAM dataset contains 220 human-human dialogs. It includes 3,044 annotated sentences in 100 dialogs.

Intent is annotated with a hierarchical scheme that first separates utterances into on-task and off-task intents. On-task intents are defined specifically for each task, while off-task intents use universal dialog-act categories: six general intents (open question, yes no question, positive answer, negative answer, responsive statement, nonresponsive statement) and six social intents (greeting, closing, apology, thanking, respond to thank, and hold).
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

The model is evaluated with automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL), Response-Intent Prediction (RIP), Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP), and Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP). RIP and RSP compare predicted response intents/slots against human-annotated ground truth, while ERIP/ERSP additionally score whether the predicted intent/slot is within an acceptable set using transition probabilities from training data. Human evaluation uses Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length, and Task Success Score, where fluency measures language quality, coherence measures logical consistency, engagement measures willingness to continue chatting, length counts dialog duration, and task success counts the average types of attacker information obtained.
Unanswerable

They compare against two state-of-the-art early rumour detection baselines: Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012), re-implemented as Liu and Yang. Both use various message-, user-, topic-, and propagation-based features with an SVM classifier.

They evaluate effectiveness using accuracy and the standard TDT evaluation procedure with official TDT3 scripts, which uses Detection Error Trade-off (DET) curves showing miss versus false alarm probability. They also evaluate efficiency by throughput per second.

Yes. They frame rumour detection as a classification problem and learn the weight vector and threshold from labeled data, using SVM training (including a two-round training procedure for the pseudo-feedback feature).
Yes. They state that because no official datasets were available, they created their own dataset, including 202 confirmed rumours and 202 non-rumours from Sina Weibo.
Chinese.

They frame rumors operationally as documents whose “likelihood of becoming a future rumour” is assessed at publication time. They also hypothesize that information not confirmed by official news is an indication of rumours.
The baselines were LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.

The paper explores three tasks: generative document evaluation, document classification, and topic2sentence. The first two are quantitative tasks, and topic2sentence is examined qualitatively through sentences generated under each topic.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese part), MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER.

They incorporate it by using relative positional encodings \(R_{t-j}\) based on the offset between target token \(t\) and context token \(j\), and adding terms involving \(R_{t-j}\) directly into the attention score: \(A^{rel}_{t,j}=Q_tK_j^T+Q_tR_{t-j}^T+uK_j^T+vR_{t-j}^T\). Because \(R_{t-j}\) uses sinusoidal functions of the relative offset, forward and backward positions differ in the sine terms while preserving distance information, allowing attention to distinguish both direction and relative distance.

Yes. The paper states that TENER achieves superior performance than prevailing BiLSTM-based models and, under the same pretrained embeddings and external knowledge, outperforms previous models on all six datasets.

SEMAFOR, SLING, and Stanford KBP are treated as the state-of-the-art systems for knowledge authoring, and a state-of-the-art machine learning approach in MetaQA [20] is the comparison for question answering. The document does not name that MetaQA machine learning approach.

They evaluated KALM-QA on two datasets: a manually constructed set of general questions based on the 50 logical frames, and the MetaQA dataset. The MetaQA dataset contains almost 29,000 test questions and over 260,000 training questions.

The paper contributes: (i) an adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence-to-sequence neural system to Italian event detection and classification in a single-step approach; (ii) an investigation of the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. The pre-trained models and scripts to run or retrain the system are also publicly available.

The paper uses FBK-HLT as the baseline, described as the best system at EVENTI Subtask B. FBK-HLT is a cascade of two SVM classifiers, one for detection and one for classification, based on rich linguistic features.
Unanswerable

They assign a cluster’s semantic concept label by first disambiguating each cluster word to the WordNet synset combination that minimizes total distance between the words’ synsets, then generating candidate labels as the union of all hypernyms of those chosen synsets, and finally ranking those candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the chosen synsets. The selected label is the candidate with the smallest total distance.

They discover coherent word clusters by first training domain-specific Word2Vec CBOW embeddings and then applying k-means to the embeddings of gender-associated words, running k-means 50 times and keeping the solution with the lowest sum of squared errors. They then exploit the cluster coherence for labeling by assigning cluster words to WordNet synsets that minimize total synset distance, generating candidate labels from the union of their hypernyms, and ranking labels by summed WordNet pathwise distance to the chosen synsets.
The two introduced datasets are the celebrity dataset with 15,917 texts and the professor dataset with 283,973 texts. They contain 342,645 and 976,677 sentences, respectively.

The authors used two strong baselines in human evaluation: a random baseline of 20% for the word intrusion task, and the centroid of the cluster as a strong baseline label for cluster labeling. The centroid baseline achieved an in-cluster rate of .60 and an out-of-cluster rate of .18.
Data-driven systems rank low in general. They often produce flirtatious or retaliatory responses, and only Neuralconvo and Cleverbot ever politely refuse.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers. After filtering, 18,826 scores remained.
Yes. The automatically annotated time expressions were evaluated: the customised HeidelTime version achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and fusional.

Yes. The morphology-level model uses the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags, and the paper specifically discusses rich derivational morphology as part of what the models must handle.

The morphological features are the outputs of gold morphological analysis: the lemma of the token followed by language-specific morphological tags. For some languages, additional information is included, such as part-of-speech tags for Turkish.
Semantic Scholar (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, Flickr30K, and COCO.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

They compared against supervised baselines using the same caption/figure classification networks initialized with random weights and trained on SciGraph from scratch, plus a frozen VGG16 ImageNet model for figure classification. For TQA, they compared with the supervised baselines from Kembhavi et al. (2017): BiDAF, TQA1 (MemoryNet/text only), TQA2 (VQA/text+image), and TQA3 (DSDP-NET/text+diagrams), as well as random.
No. While some corpora are specific to the scientific domain (SciGraph, SemScholar), the paper also uses general-purpose data such as Wikipedia, Flickr30K, and COCO.

Their figures and captions come from the Semantic Scholar corpus: they downloaded 3.3M PDFs and extracted 12.5M figures and captions using PDFFigures2. For some experiments, they also used the intersection of Springer Nature’s SciGraph with Semantic Scholar, yielding 82K figures.
Unanswerable
No, they explicitly state that their model predicts emotion intensity “without leveraging on lexicons.”
The baseline was the Weka baseline (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a).

Their intra-sentence attention RNN outperformed the Weka baseline on the development set but performed worse on the test set. The average Pearson correlation was 0.689 on development and 0.522 on test, compared to the baseline’s 0.611 on development and 0.648 on test.

The training, validation, and test datasets provided for the EmoInt-2017 shared task were used, consisting of tweets annotated with Best-Worst Scaling for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.

Hate speech is described as language that “promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups” and can incite hate crime. Their annotation schema further treats it as including whether a text is offensive, disrespectful, hateful, fearful out of ignorance, abusive, or normal, and what attribute or group it discriminates against.
English, French, and Arabic.
The aspects considered are the tweet’s directness, hostility type, target attribute, target group, and the annotator’s sentiment.
The dataset contains a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language, plus 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets.

Semantic Web technologies offer opportunities in MT by improving semantic and syntactic disambiguation, named entity recognition and linking, and adaptation to non-standard speech through structured knowledge, ontologies, reasoning, SPARQL queries, and user-context data. They can also support translating and enriching knowledge bases across languages, generating bilingual ontologies or embeddings, and then using those translated KBs as external resources or in language models to improve entity and domain-specific translation.

The paper states that although Semantic Web technologies can improve MT, their use is still in its infancy and they have mostly been applied only at the semantic analysis step. It also notes practical challenges such as tedious implementation for common-word disambiguation, the poor run-time performance of reasoners, and the fact that many knowledge bases are mostly in English, limiting their contribution to MT for other languages.

Besides lexical and syntactic ambiguity, the paper mentions complex semantic ambiguity, structural divergence, and linguistic properties/features. It also lists five generic open challenges: overfocus on English/European languages and low-resource pairs, SMT limitations across domains, translating social-network non-standard text, difficult translation among morphologically rich languages, and mismatches between speech-translation training data and real user speech.

The EEG features used were five statistical features: root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis, and power spectral entropy. In total, this gave 155 EEG features from 31 channels × 5 features.

The baselines were two end-to-end ASR system models: an attention model and a Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) model. The results compared EEG-only input against concatenated EEG + MFCC acoustic features.
Unanswerable
No. In Table 3, all LadaBERT variants have lower accuracy than the teacher BERT-Base on every reported task.

Yes. They compare weight pruning, matrix factorization, and hybrid pruning in Table 3 and conclude that the combination of matrix factorization and weight pruning is better than single solutions, with hybrid pruning generally outperforming pure matrix factorization and often pure weight pruning.
SST-2, MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, QNLI, and QQP.
They are working with news text, specifically datasets created from DailyMail news and evaluation on DUC-2002 documents covering various news topics.

They trained on two datasets created from DailyMail news. They evaluated on the DUC-2002 single-document summarization task and on 500 articles from the DailyMail test set, and also report ROUGE on the entire DailyMail test set.

Yes, they include a neural abstractive summarization baseline (NN-ABS) in both automatic and human evaluations. They report that NN-WE consistently outperforms the purely abstractive model, and that NN-SE is significantly better than NN-ABS in human evaluation.

They are talking about commonsense knowledge in the form of ontologies of hierarchical data, especially transitive entailment relations such as IS-A relations. The paper also mentions non-hierarchical commonsense knowledge from raw text, but its main focus is hierarchical commonsense ontologies.

They mean representation spaces whose geometric structure directly encodes structural constraints, specifically partial-order or lattice relations, so predictions obey those constraints automatically. In this paper, this is realized by order embeddings where a concept x is below concept y iff every coordinate of x is greater than or equal to the corresponding coordinate of y, so general concepts are smaller than specific concepts in every embedding dimension.
Yes. The paper states that its targeted and untargeted sentiment analysis models were pre-trained on larger corpora and then evaluated directly on the SEC task without further adaptation.

The models extracted word unigrams and bigrams (TF-IDF), distributed semantic features from pretrained embeddings (including 300-dimensional word2vec, and experiments with GloVe and Sent2Vec), and sentiment features from manually constructed lexica plus pre-trained sentiment embeddings. The lexicon-based sentiment features included word percentages across 192 lexical categories using Empath, including emotion classes such as surprise and disgust.
Unanswerable

The two multimodal resources are AI2D and AI2D-RST. AI2D contains crowd-sourced annotations, while AI2D-RST contains expert annotations built on top of the crowd-sourced descriptions for a 1000-diagram subset of AI2D.

The crowd-sourced annotations were created by non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk, whereas AI2D-RST was annotated by trained experts. The document does not state whether the annotators were familiar with the science topics.

They are compared through two experiments that evaluate AI2D (crowd-sourced) and AI2D-RST (expert) annotations on diagram and node classification using graph neural networks. Performance is measured with metrics such as macro F1 and differences between AI2D and the best AI2D-RST graph are tested over 20 runs using the Mann-Whitney U test.
Amazon Mechanical Turk.
They are the annotators who annotated the AI2D-RST subset of 1000 diagrams using a new multi-layer annotation schema. Five annotators are mentioned in the inter-annotator agreement evaluation.
They use two model architectures: a Recurrent Neural Network, specifically the Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN), and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).

They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset of [4] for baseline comparison, and for cross-temporal, cross-topic, and cross-domain experiments they generate a new dataset based on Wikipedia crawl data. This new dataset is seeded from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages from 2018 and 2009.
Unanswerable

They use a Wikipedia-derived weak-signal dataset: controversial seeds come from Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” pages (2018 and 2009), negatives from Wikipedia’s random article endpoint, and then they crawl “See also,” “References,” and “External links” up to two hops. Labels are propagated from these seeds to the linked pages, including non-Wikipedia general web pages.
Yes. They compare their semantic/neural approaches against lexical full-text baselines including TfIdf-SVM and language models (LM-DBPedia, LM-wiki), as well as other previous methods.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2B tokens, were used for training.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos, comprising a total of 1.2B tokens.

The videos were represented by 1500-dimensional frame-level visual feature vectors extracted from video frames at 1-second intervals, similar to features used for large-scale image classification tasks.
Yes. The experiments used an LSTM-based NMT architecture “implementing the attention mechanism,” specifically the “scaled Luong” attention mechanism.
An established neural machine translation architecture based on LSTMs with attention was explored, specifically the Luong et al. (2017) model using the “scaled Luong” attention mechanism.

Symbolic rewriting is rewriting symbolic terms from one form to another according to equations or rewrite rules, e.g. pairs of terms \(u[\theta(s)]\) and \(u[\theta(t)]\) rewritten from one to another with \(s=t\). In this paper, it is studied in a formal-to-formal setting on terms represented as linear sequences of tokens.

They incorporate expert knowledge by adding anchor variables—typically expert-chosen words or features—to latent factors through a mutual-information term, β∑(i,j)∈R I(Xi; Yj), in the objective. This lets experts seed topics and impose semantics, for example by anchoring a “diabetes” topic to the word “diabetes,” after which the model discovers other associated words and includes them in that topic.
20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 2008 Obesity Challenge dataset.
No. The paper mentions SeededLDA in related work, but the reported experiments compare Anchored CorEx to Unsupervised CorEx rather than against popular topic models such as LDA.
54.82 overall micro F1-score.

Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media. The paper’s integrated training model, F-Score Driven Model II, achieves a new state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese social media.
Sina Weibo.
Chinese social media text with a limited labeled corpus and massive unlabeled text.
XNLI: Cross-lingual NLI; MLDoc: Document Classification; Cross-lingual Intent Classification; POS Tagging; Named Entity Recognition.

Yes. The paper states that “the gains are more pronounced in low resource languages,” and the abstract also notes that improved translation performance on low-resource languages hints at stronger cross-lingual transfer capability.

No. The paper describes mBERT as pre-trained on monolingual data in 104 languages within a single model, and discusses zero-shot cross-lingual transfer rather than fine-tuning mBERT separately for each language.

They did not select a fixed set of 50 languages directly; the experiments cover over 50 languages across 5 tasks. For POS tagging, they used 48 languages chosen as the intersection of languages with POS labels in Universal Dependencies v2.3 and the languages supported by their mNMT model.

They use both automatic and human evaluations. Automatic evaluation uses perplexity and BLEU against human-annotated responses, while human evaluation uses Likert-style judgments and ACUTE-EVAL self-chat A/B comparisons on full dialogues for engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.
Unanswerable

They use a translation-based pipeline with Poly-encoder adapted via Google Translate API: target-language query → English query → English response → target-language response. The model is denoted as Poly.
The newly created dataset contains Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Japanese, and Korean.

Yes. They automatically created their own contrastive test set from the OpenSubtitles corpus by extracting suitable examples and then introducing contrastive translations by swapping the correct pronoun with an incorrect one.

The baselines are standard context-agnostic sentence-level models: for RNNs, a bidirectional RNN with attention trained with Nematus; for Transformers, a standard Transformer-base. The paper also considers simple concatenation baselines, concat22 for both architectures and concat21 for the Transformer, where the previous sentence is concatenated to the current one in the training data.

The experimented context-aware models were recurrent concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, and s-hier-to-2, and Transformer concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) source-context Transformer. They also tested weight tying variants for each recurrent model.
English→German.

They use a simple entity linking algorithm that directly links a mention to the entity with the greatest commonness score, where commonness is computed from Wikipedia anchor links as the probability of an entity given the mention string. They also use this commonness score as the confidence of the linking result, and within the same document they apply a heuristic from Ganea and Hofmann (2017) to corefer generic person mentions to more specific mentions.

They use two layers of BiLSTMs for the context, then concatenate the context, mention-string, KB-type, and entity-linking confidence representations and feed them into an MLP with three dense layers. The final mention representation is scored against embedded type vectors using dot products.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN. OntoNotes is mentioned but not used.
1 domain expert.
The evaluation uses precision, recall, f1-score/F1-score, accuracy, and accuracy without the 'O'-tag. The paper reports an overall F1-score of 0.89 with an 80–20 train/evaluation split.

The fine-tuned BERT model achieves an F1-score of 0.89 on the concept recognition dataset, with overall accuracy of 0.97 and accuracy without the 'O'-tag of 0.86. The micro/macro-average precision, recall, and F1 are 0.90, 0.88, and 0.88, respectively.
Yes. The paper states, “Implementation with demo and dataset is available at: https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.”
It uses a BIO labelling scheme. The tags shown are O plus B-/I- tags for these concept types: abb, grp, syscon, opcon, seterm, event, org, art, cardinal, loc, and mea.
BERT's pre-trained cased language model.
The constructed CR dataset contains roughly 3700 annotated sentences.
Precision, recall and F-measure.
Unanswerable

The paper uses learned word2vec-based embeddings: averaged word vectors, paragraph vectors (PV-DM/ParaVec), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE). The embeddings are trained on corpora including ACL+AZ, MixedAbs, and the Brown corpus.
No. They use the existing Argumentative Zoning Corpus, which consists of 80 AZ-annotated conference articles.

Sentence embeddings are generated using three explored models: by averaging the word vectors in a sentence, by training paragraph vectors with the PV-DM paragraph2vec model, and by training BAS-specific word embeddings and then averaging those word vectors in the sentence. The averaging model uses word embeddings learned by the classical word2vec algorithm.

Argumentative zoning (AZ), also called rhetorical sentence classification, is the process of assigning rhetorical status to extracted sentences in scientific articles. It provides readers with general discourse context so that scientific ideas can be better linked, compared, and analyzed.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites, YouLikeHits and Like4Like, from February to April 2019, created dummy accounts on them, and recorded the Tweet IDs of tweets posted for gaining retweets. They then used Twitter's REST API to collect the tweet objects, and also collected the authors' timelines to obtain genuine tweets by the same users that were not posted to those blackmarket sites.

They compare against state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods and standard classifiers on their features: Wu et al. [5] using Word2Vec/Doc2Vec with an MLP, Rajdev et al. [12] using their setup with a Random Forest, and feature concatenation with Tweet2Vec plus tweet content features fed to Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English

They look at freemium blackmarket services, specifically three categories: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services. They contrast these with the broader premium vs. freemium models, but mainly focus on freemium services.
They use English and Chinese for the two-language pretraining setting, and English, French, and Chinese for the three-language pretraining setting.

The decoder is part of a Transformer-based sequence-to-sequence model. It generates the output sequence in the target language by conditioning on the encoder’s vector representations and a specific language tag.

It is a Transformer-based bidirectional encoder. It encodes multilingual input sentences into vector representations, uses language tag embeddings to distinguish languages, and is pretrained with MLM and XMLM to map multilingual sentences into a shared embedding space.
The baselines are COREFNQG, MP-GSN, and XLM for English-English question generation; and XLM plus PIPELINE (XLM) for zero-shot Chinese-Chinese question generation.

They look at prompt adherence via a human triple pairing task, where judges select the correct prompt-story pairing among shuffled groups of three stories. They also use human preference judgments in a blind test comparing hierarchical generation from a prompt against generation without a prompt.
Perplexity and prompt ranking accuracy.

Language model baselines: non-hierarchical story generation models that do not condition on the prompt, using both the gated convolutional language model (GCNN) of Dauphin et al. (2017) and their additional self-attention mechanism. Seq2seq baselines: LSTM and convolutional seq2seq architectures, plus Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention; they also compare to an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models and a KNN retrieval baseline using TF-IDF prompt vectors with FASTTEXT and FAISS, returning the closest training-story truncated to 150 words.
No. The paper mentions a pretrained Conv seq2seq with self-attention model for fusion training, not pre-trained embeddings like BERT.
The premise is generated using the convolutional language model from Dauphin et al. (2017).
Yes.
From Reddit’s WRITINGPROMPTS forum.
word2vec, fastText, GloVe, Baroni, and SL999.

The datasets used were SICK, STSB, and MRPC (Microsoft Research Paraphrase Corpus). SICK was used for textual entailment recognition and also for semantic textual similarity, STSB was used for semantic textual similarity, and MRPC was used for paraphrase identification.

The state-of-the-art methods mentioned for comparison in textual similarity tasks are Tai et al. (2015)’s Dependency Tree-Structured LSTM, Wu et al. (2017)’s hybrid linear regression model with WordNet, alignment features, and word2vec, and Tian et al. (2017)’s ensemble boosting model combining traditional features with sentence modeling methods. The paper also discusses He et al. (2015)’s multi-granularity CNN approach as a strong prior method.
Fleiss's Kappa for inter-annotator agreement.
Yes. They report inter-annotator agreement using Fleiss’s Kappa, with values shown in Table 3 for each criterion and cyberbullying.

The social-network features are five neighborhood-overlap measures between author and target in the directed following network: downward overlap, upward overlap, inward overlap, outward overlap, and bidirectional overlap. They are modified Jaccard similarity indices over follower/following neighborhoods.
aggression (AGGR), repetition (REP), harmful intent (HARM), visibility among peers (PEER), and power imbalance (POWER).

Cyberbullying is defined in the paper as involving a subset of five criteria: aggression, repetition, harmful intent, visibility among peers, and power imbalance. The authors note that existing definitions vary, but all available definitions they reviewed contained a strict subset of these criteria.

The output was evaluated objectively for similarity to the training data using Phrase Overlap match and 4-gram Jaccard similarity, subjectively for humor/content quality using human judgment, and for syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser. They also reported overall syntactic accuracy as the percentage of sentences with at least one valid linkage at Null Count 0.
The joke data came from two sources: CrowdTruth and Subreddits.
Inspirational quotes.

On FB15k-237, CapsE outperforms the closely related state-of-the-art ConvKB by 0.105 MRR (about 25.1% relative improvement) and 6.1% absolute Hits@10. It also obtains the best mean rank on WN18RR and the highest MRR and Hits@10 on FB15k-237.
No. The experiments described use BERT-based models only, although the introduction mentions other pre-trained models such as Open-GPT, ERNIE, GPT2.0, and MT-DNN.

They compared BERT-only with BERT + Bi-LSTM for sequence labeling; DenseNet and HighwayLSTM for text classification; and BERT-only, BERT + BIMPM, BERT + BIMPM with the first bi-LSTM removed, and BERT + Sim-Transformer for semantic similarity.
CoNLL03, Yahoo Answer Classification Dataset, and Quora-Question-Pair dataset.

They obtained high inter-annotator agreement: Phase 1 raw agreement was .92 (scene), .95 (function), .90 (construal) with Cohen’s kappa .90, .93, .88; Phase 2 raw agreement was .93, .90, .89 with kappa .92, .88, .88.

They annotated the Chinese translation of The Little Prince after preprocessing: automatic Jieba tokenization with manual correction so potential adpositions were separate tokens, then all annotators jointly manually identified adposition targets according to their criteria, since automatic POS tagging was unsuitable. The corpus was jointly annotated by three native Mandarin Chinese speakers with advanced linguistics training; 25% of adposition targets were labeled cooperatively, and the rest were labeled independently by all three annotators and then cooperatively adjudicated, with annotations stored in CoNLL-U-Lex alongside StanfordNLP POS tags and dependency trees.
The corpus contains 27 chapters, 1,597 sentences, and 20,287 tokens.

They use a training dataset with five features: ID, title, author, text, and label, containing 20,800 samples. The provided test dataset has no labels, so they do not use it; instead, they randomly select a test set from the training data for evaluation.

The paper explores TF-IDF, CountVectorizer, and Word2Vec as pre-training/vectorization models, and Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), LSTMs, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) as fine-tuning models. It reports that a combination of CountVectorizer with LSTM achieves the best performance.

The essay features are extracted in six groups: numerical features, language-model probabilities, word-cluster representations, topic-model representations, part-of-speech tag features, and essay-text features. Specifically, these include counts such as sentences, words, letters, Gunning Fog index, difficult/misspelled/duplicate words, and low-idf words; probabilities under two trigram language models; binary one-hot word-cluster vectors; concatenated LDA topic distributions; POS unigram bag-of-words; and an idf-weighted bigram bag-of-words representation of the essay text.
The evaluation metrics were the challenge’s official error measure E and accuracy. The paper reports stratified 3-fold cross-validation scores for both metrics.
The model used is gradient boosted trees with gradient-based one-side sampling, implemented in the Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM) toolkit.

Future work is to investigate whether the usefulness of LDA topic-distribution features reflects true modeling power or an artifact/data leak from essay prompts tied to candidate level. The paper suggests further analysis and validation, specifically using essay topics to create validation splits once those topics and test data are released.
For reference, the baseline was a tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for class imbalance. It achieved error 10.10 and accuracy 97.2 using all features.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated by manual evaluation of text-to-text alignments, averaged over 90 items and two raters. Ratings are analyzed by hunalign confidence bins, and inter-annotator agreement is measured with Krippendorff’s α-reliability score.

The speech alignment quality is evaluated by manual evaluation of audio-text alignment. Two annotators rated 90 items in total, 30 from each bin, on a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment, 2 = partial alignment, 3 = correct alignment; inter-annotator reliability was measured with Krippendorff’s α and was 1.00 for audio-text alignment.

Yes. They explicitly state that unlike selective attention, their selective gate is designed to handle bags with only one sentence by assigning a low gating value to wrongly labeled instances, noting that about 80% of bags contain only one sentence.

Yes. In the case study, Table 6 is “a case study where each bag contains one sentence,” and Bags 1 and 2 are shown as correct predictions by their method while “without the support of the self-attention enhanced selective gate, the model will misclassify both bags into NA.”
Unanswerable

They evaluate on the standard SICK (Sentences Involving Compositional Knowledge) dataset, consisting of about 10,000 English sentence pairs. They use it for two tasks: semantic relatedness and textual entailment.

No. They only report end-task results for full models such as WordEmbedding+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN and CharCNN+MB-LSTM+Temp-CNN, and discuss comparisons like Tree LSTM vs Sequence LSTM, but do not separately evaluate the learned representations before the CNN layer.
Unanswerable
SRE18 development and SRE18 evaluation datasets.
The eftdnn subsystem outperformed the others.

No. The paper says that adding up frequencies of synonyms and semantically related words generally degrades the model and causes overgeneralized topics, though directing added frequencies toward more specific hyponyms/longer ngrams can improve topic quality.

They explore economic and political domains with EuroVoc, and the banking domain with RuThes. They also use general English and Russian text collections including Europarl, JRC-Acquis, ACL Anthology, NIPS Conference Papers, and Russian banking texts.
They used three thesauri: WordNet, the European Union information-retrieval thesaurus EuroVoc, and the Russian thesaurus RuThes.

They define hate speech as language used to express hatred toward a targeted group or intended to be derogatory, humiliate, or insult members of the group. In extreme cases, it may also threaten or incite violence, but they do not limit the definition only to such cases.
A logistic regression model with L2 regularization in a one-versus-rest framework.
33,458 Twitter users.
The dataset contains 24,802 labeled tweets. It was drawn from a corpus of 85.4 million tweets collected from 33,458 Twitter users.
SST-2, specifically for the large variants of BERT and RoBERTa. In these cases, quality consistently increases when freezing 12–16 layers, so fine-tuning all layers hurts performance.
Yes. They study both base and large variants of RoBERTa, including RoBERTaLARGE.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable

It mitigates the bias pattern by making hypothesis artifacts balanced across labels through sample reweighting, using weights based on \(P(y|h)\) so artifacts become unpredictable to the model. In practice, it uses smoothed weights \(w = 1/((1-\epsilon)P(y|h)+\epsilon)\) and normalizes label-wise total weights equally to keep the label prior unchanged.
They use SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI, and SICK. For the cross-dataset evaluation, models are trained on SNLI and MultiNLI and tested on SICK and JOCI.
Entropy.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
The IDN Tagged Corpus contains 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.

The neural network models explored were feedforward and bidirectional LSTM (biLSTM) taggers, each paired with either a softmax or a CRF prediction layer: Feedforward + softmax, Feedforward + CRF, biLSTM + softmax, and biLSTM + CRF. They also examined feature variants for the best neural tagger, adding character, prefix, and suffix features.

The rule-based model evaluated was the rule-based tagger designed by Rashel et al. It tags named entities and multiword expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag the remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted rules to resolve ambiguous tags.
The task uses the IDN Tagged Corpus. It contains 10K sentences and 250K manually tagged tokens.
They train the embeddings on parts of Chinese Wikipedia articles, resulting in 11,529,432 segmented words.
No. They evaluate the embeddings only on word similarity and word analogy tasks.
Unanswerable
The paper identifies a need for flexibility in out-of-vocabulary words, and notes that there is an effort to resolve this issue.

Yes. Section 3.4 states that “POS and morph classes” are included in the results, and Section 1.5 says “the tagset with the morphological features was used” and reports precision, recall, and F1 scores.
The proposed model extends the standard ENAMEX types of organization, location, and person by adding a fourth class: facility (FAC).
The extracted morphological features are gender, number, and case.
Yes. The reported results are on a held-out test set of Twitter users from a corpus of publicly available tweets about pregnancy on Twitter, and no non-English datasets are mentioned.

The dataset consists of a sample of 10,417 Twitter users drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who had announced their pregnancy on Twitter. Two professional annotators labeled users as “bot,” “non-bot,” or “unavailable” based on their publicly available Twitter sites, with κ = 0.93 inter-annotator agreement on 1,000 overlapping annotations. After resolving disagreements, 413 users (4%) were labeled bot, 7,849 (75.35%) non-bot, and 2,069 (19.9%) unavailable.

By extending a political bot detector such as Botometer with a statistical machine learning classifier and additional health-specific features. The paper adds features including tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topics, mean post length, profile picture face detection, and presence of a person’s name in the username, and uses these with a gradient boosting classifier plus SMOTE to improve performance on health-related Twitter users.

Adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, and characterizing drug abuse. It also mentions longitudinal user-level analysis, such as mining health-related information from cohorts of pregnant women.

No, the QA tuples do not fall under a single specific domain. The paper uses a customer support dataset sourced from Twitter containing conversations with at least one question and one free-form answer.
The baseline model is the fine-tuned multilingual BERT model without emoji tokenization or special emoji support.
The corpus contains 2,000 QA tuples.
They used a Twitter customer support dataset: 2000 tuples collected by Hori and Hori, sourced from Twitter.
A Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM) algorithm was used, implementing fast gradient boosting on decision trees. It combined predictions from base classifiers with TF-IDF and QIEF input features.
Yes. They compared two text embeddings: the original general BERT (Bert-Base) and BioBERT, and found BioBERT performed better.
363,078 structured abstracts.

The new dataset, PICONET, was collected from structured PubMed abstracts by searching on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 English human clinical trial abstracts, then selecting section headings that uniquely represented Population/Problem, Intervention, and Outcome. The authors lemmatized section labels to cluster similar headings, manually inspected samples to choose representative labels, kept full abstract sections instead of splitting them into sentences, added multilabel sections and negative examples from headings like “aim,” and cleaned the data with language identification, removal of missing values and unicode issues, and filtering to sequences of 5–200 words.

The dataset was automatically labeled from structured PubMed abstract headings; no specific annotators are named. The authors only state that they manually looked at a small number of samples for each label to determine if the text was representative.

Previous datasets used a keyword-based approach that produced ambiguous and redundant labels, with sections like “population and methods” labeled as P even though they also contained intervention and study-design information. They also split multi-sentence sections into individual P sentences, which the authors say can create poor candidates for the label and lose richer contextual features.
Unanswerable

They explore four agreement relation types: subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive adjective agreement, and subject-predicate adjective agreement. They examine these relations across the features of number, gender, case, and person.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, and TREC.
Their approach is compared against the baseline TextFooler. They evaluate attacks on three text classification models: word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.
By 0.037 F1: their approach achieved 0.752 versus the prior best 0.715 on the same Amazon dataset.
Buschmeier, Cimiano, and Klinger (2014). Their model achieved an F1 of 0.713 on the same Amazon review target domain.
The baseline models are the All Sarcasm baseline, which assumes every instance is sarcastic, and the Random baseline, which randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic.
Twitter and Amazon product reviews.

Data was taken from two domains: Twitter and Amazon product reviews. The Amazon reviews came from the publicly available sarcasm corpus developed by Filatova (2012), and the Twitter dataset was built from tweets collected in February and March 2016 containing exactly one trailing hashtag: “#sarcasm” for sarcastic tweets and one of “#happiness,” “#sadness,” “#anger,” “#surprise,” “#fear,” or “#disgust” for non-sarcastic tweets.
The best model, LSTM and GRU with Attention, achieved an F1-macro of 0.89.

The models tested on the dataset include deep learning models such as Pooled GRU and LSTMs with GRU and attention, as well as a BERT architecture. The classical machine learning models mentioned are linear SVM, SGDC, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes.
LSTM and GRU with Attention performs best on the offensive language identification task, achieving an F1-macro of 0.89.
No. The dataset was annotated by a team of three volunteers, with disagreements resolved by one of the authors and two extra human judges.
3 annotators.
No. The benchmark annotated dataset contained 4,779 tweets with over 29% offensive content, so it was not balanced.

They experiment with classical machine learning models including SGDC, Multinomial Naïve Bayes, Bernoulli Naïve Bayes, and linear SVM, as well as six deep learning models. The deep learning models explicitly mentioned are Pooled GRU, LSTM and GRU with Attention, and a BERT architecture.
Unanswerable
568 reviews in Catalan and 343 reviews in Basque, for a total of 911 reviews.
3 annotators.

The sentence construction component relies on the GF Library. It uses the GF grammar produced by the first component, and the GF libraries concatenate the provided strings according to the target language grammar to create a complete sentence.

The two use cases are: generating natural language descriptions for annotated ontologies/workflows, including the Phylotastic project and a people ontology; and creating an intermediate representation for Wikipedia pages that can be translated, evaluated by regenerating English from the representation.

Yes. They examine the structure of the learned language vectors and report that these representations capture similarity and genetic relationships between languages, including clustering Germanic languages in ways that largely match established linguistic relationships.

A standard stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a final softmax output layer. They concatenate language embedding vectors to the inputs of the LSTMs at each time step and to the hidden layer before the softmax.

They show genetic relationships between languages by hierarchically clustering the learned language vectors. For a subset of Germanic languages, this clustering closely matches the established genetic relationships in that language family.

No. They state in the abstract that MagiCoder reduces the time required for encoding ADR reports, but the reported testing was an automated comparison against manually revised reports from experts, not a study on real pharmacologists measuring time.

Yes. They suggest that longer descriptions do not usually have a complex structure, but MagiCoder performs only pure syntactical, word-by-word recognition and does not take into account sentence structure or semantic information, which likely hurts performance more on longer texts.

The MagiCoder algorithm has five phases: definition of ad hoc data structures; preprocessing of the original text; word-by-word linear scan of the description with a voting task; weights calculation; and sorting of voted terms followed by release of winning terms. In more detail, preprocessing includes tokenization, stemming, and stop-word removal; the scan lets each word vote for LLTs it belongs to and stores matching information; then terms are weighted, pruned and sorted, and a final subset of winning terms is selected using heuristic criteria.

The system is made linear by preprocessing the terminology once into hash-table meta-dictionaries (DictByWord and DictByWordStem) that map each word to the set of LLTs containing it, built in O(mk), so checking whether a narrative token appears in the terminology is constant time. Then MagiCoder performs a single word-by-word scan of the narrative description, updating voted terms through these direct accesses rather than approximate matching, so the overall worst-case complexity is O(nm), with the selection phase typically working only on the smaller subset of voted terms.

They aim to draw “a more complete picture of the aspects and mechanisms of personal recovery in bipolar disorder” by complementing prior qualitative interviews and standardized questionnaires with computational analysis of large-scale first-person social media accounts. The authors conclude this should broaden understanding beyond mainly English-speaking Western participants and provide insights informed by a more heterogeneous, multilingual population.
Unanswerable

The analysis used a mixed-methods explanatory sequential design, beginning with exploratory quantitative statistical analysis of the full dataset and followed by manual qualitative investigation of selected examples. Computational linguistic methods included comparative corpus analysis with Wmatrix to identify keywords and key concepts, an iterative recovery-term dictionary, automatic semantic tagging with the UCREL Semantic Analysis System (USAS), and distributional semantics methods to find frequently co-occurring terms.
No. The paper states that they will not ask the Twitter and Reddit account holders included in their datasets for consent.

Twitter and Reddit users with bipolar disorder will be identified automatically via self-reported diagnosis statements, such as “I was diagnosed with BD-I last week,” using extended diagnosis patterns and terms from Cohan et al. (2018). Relevant blogs will be manually identified, and their authors will be contacted to obtain informed consent for using their texts.
The training data comes from CCPC1.0. The paper also states they do not use contemporary Chinese news corpus for pre-training.
SHI and CI. Specifically, the generated major types are SHI in the forms of Jueju and Lvshi, and CI in 121 major forms (Cipai).

They compare primarily to subword/BPE NMT from Sennrich, Haddow, and Birch (2015b), and also to a fully character-based system implementing Lee, Cho, and Hofmann (2016). In related work they also discuss hybrid character-word NMT from Luong and Manning (2016) and large target-vocabulary methods from Jean et al. (2014) and Mi, Wang, and Ittycheriah (2016).
Yes. In the training details, they state: “For SG and CBOW, we set the vector dimensionality to 100,” indicating they use skip-gram word2vec.

Quality is measured by evaluation tasks on the learned word vectors: word similarity, synonym selection, and word analogy. Specifically, the paper uses Pearson correlation between human similarity judgments and cosine distances for word similarity, chooses the candidate with highest cosine similarity in synonym selection, and evaluates analogy performance by selecting the word maximizing cosine similarity to \(v_{w_b}-v_{w_a}+v_{w_c}\).

Yes. The reported results are on KPTimes and JPTimes news datasets and on existing benchmark datasets such as DUC, KPCrowd, SemEval, and KP20k, all described as news or scientific article datasets, with no mention of non-English data.
They were collected by crawling selected online news websites, primarily The New York Times. A secondary source is the Japan Times.
MultipartiteRank.

Two generative neural keyphrase generation models are trained: CopySci and CopyNews. They are both CopyRNN encoder-decoder models with a copying mechanism, trained respectively on scientific abstracts (KP20k) and newspaper articles (KPTimes).

The editors’ annotations are more uniform and consistent than those in existing datasets, using a more controlled indexing vocabulary with frequent keyphrases closer to topic descriptors, whereas non-expert annotators use a larger, less controlled vocabulary. Editors are also more likely to generalize and assign keyphrases that do not occur in the document, resulting in a higher proportion of missing keyphrases (about 55%).
This results in a vocabulary size of 29,044.
Yes. They use word embeddings for context words learned through the unsupervised GloVe approach.
The paper proposes a single Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory network, i.e., one single deep BLSTM network. Unanswerable
F-measure (%). They also refer to accuracy in terms of F-measure.
They used four datasets: the DUC-2001 dataset, the Inspec dataset, the NUS Keyphrase Corpus, and the ICSI Meeting Corpus.
27.53 on DUC, 27.01 on Inspec, 4.30 on ICSI, and 9.10 on Nus.
SingleRank and Topical PageRank are the state-of-the-art models used for comparison. The paper reports that WikiRank outperforms both of them.

They do not directly verify that the word alignments are correct. The paper only states that ambiguous attention distributions are often associated with untranslated meaningful source words, and therefore it uses the ART teacher’s encoder-to-decoder attention as word-alignment hints by minimizing KL divergence so the student has similar alignments to the teacher.

The paper says ART models suffer from high inference latency because they generate tokens sequentially during decoding. It states the proposed model is one order of magnitude faster in inference than a strong LSTM-based ART baseline.
BLEU score.
Yes. Besides WMT14, the model was also evaluated on the IWSLT14 German-to-English dataset.
No. The experiments are only reported on German-English and English-German datasets: IWSLT14 De-En and WMT14 En-De/De-En.

The most discriminating features are average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation, with the highest information gain obtained by average, maximum, minimum, and standard deviation. Probability and proportionality features have low information gain.

On the alternate dataset (the DSLCC development set), the proposed low dimensionality representation obtained results comparable to Skip-gram and SenVec, supporting its robustness. Specifically, LDR scores were 99.9 for Bulgarian and Macedonian, 84.7 for Spain Spanish, 88.0 for Argentina Spanish, 87.4 for Portugal Portuguese, 90.0 for Brazilian Portuguese, 78.0 for Bosnian, 85.8 for Croatian, 86.4 for Serbian, 99.4 for Indonesian, 99.2 for Malay, 99.8 for Czech, 99.3 for Slovak, and 99.9 for Other languages.

No. The paper says extracting answer spans by looking for occurrences of the gold standard answer in the provided snippets “is not perfect” and can produce false negatives when “the exact string used is not in the synonym list,” but it does not report the coverage of answers being substrings.
Unanswerable

The paper uses SGNN for script event prediction, replacing its input event embeddings with the proposed intent and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings. For sentiment-based prediction, it uses a softmax binary classifier.

It is evaluated as a script event prediction task on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset, where given an existing event context, the system chooses the most reasonable subsequent event from a candidate list. Performance is reported by accuracy improvement over baselines.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset (including Weber et al. (2018)’s 230 event-pair set and the authors’ extended dataset), the transitive sentence similarity dataset, and the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset.
No. The comparisons reported are against PPMI-SVD, SGNS, and their own character-level variants (C2V-NO-ATT and CHAR2VEC), with no mention of fastText or other subword-information models.
Unanswerable
English.

They are evaluated by ranking word pairs according to the cosine similarity of their word vectors and then measuring the correlation with human similarity judgments using Spearman’s ρ. The comparison uses the WordSim353 dataset, the MEN test split, and the Rare Word dataset.
15.6/16.5 (accuracy/NDCG) on MCTest-150 and 7.3/4.6 on MCTest-500.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, NR (Neural Reasoner), and AR (Attentive Reader). The section also states that Addition and Addition-proj are simple baselines without complex composition and inference, while NR and AR are top-performing deep neural networks for QA tasks.

The baseline is a one-stage RNN system with the same RNN structure as the last stage: a 2-layer BLSTM directly trained to recognize dialect category. In the comparison table, the baseline uses the same ResNet14 architecture and BLSTM RNN as the multi-stage systems.

The two-stage and three-stage systems both improve relative accuracy by about 10% over the baseline on every task, with the two-stage system performing best. Their accuracies are: baseline 78.85% (all), 77.60% (≤3s), 80.10% (>3s); two-stage 88.88%, 87.72%, 90.04%; three-stage 87.24%, 85.52%, 88.96%.
Ningxia, Hefei, Sichuan, Shanxi, Changsha, Hebei, Nanchang, Shanghai, Kekka, and Fujian.
An ensemble of four Transformer neural machine translation models was used.
Unanswerable

The restricted system does not use any external NLP tools, spell checkers, or hand-crafted confusion sets, and relies solely on neural sequence models. It uses standard Transformer implementations with minimal preprocessing (subword segmentation).
Unanswerable
There is no clear winner according to human evaluation: MODULAR is better at reducing bias and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT is more fluent, preserves meaning better, and has higher BLEU.

They were Wikipedia edits by English Wikipedia editors, not edits produced by trained annotators. The corpus originates from Wikipedia edits that removed various framings, presuppositions, and attitudes from biased sentences.

Subjective text is automatically neutralized by translating an input sentence into an output sentence that preserves meaning while removing subjective bias. The paper proposes two neural approaches: a MODULAR system with BERT-based detection of biased words followed by LSTM-based editing, and a CONCURRENT encoder-decoder system that directly generates a neutralized sentence from the subjective source.
Recognizing American Sign Language signs from multimodal data, specifically using skeletal and RGB video data across 51 signs.
Spatial AI-LSTM is the best model, with 81% cross-subject accuracy.
